title: Measuring Embedding Distance
concept: Cosine similarity measures the angle between word vectors, checking semantic closeness independent of vector length.

exercises.prompt: Before measuring distance, why do we use word embeddings in AI?

options.text: To represent word meanings as vectors (lists of numbers)
To count how many letters are in a sentence

exercises.explain: Word embeddings convert words into vectors (lists of numbers) so that computers can process their meanings.

cards.title: How Do Computers Compare Words?
cards.body: We know that computers turn words into lists of numbers called embeddings. But how does a computer actually compare them to know that 'puppy' and 'dog' mean almost the same thing? We need a way to measure the distance between these word directions!

cards.title: What is Cosine Similarity?
cards.body: Cosine similarity is a method that measures the angle between two word vectors in space. Instead of measuring the straight-line distance, it checks if the vectors are pointing in a similar direction. A value close to 1 means they point in nearly the same direction (highly similar), while a value close to 0 means they are unrelated.

cards.title: The Compass Arrows
cards.body: Think of two arrows representing arrows of interest. If you and a friend are pointing toward 'Outer Space' and 'Stars', your arrows point in almost the exact same direction (a small angle). If you point to 'Cooking' and your friend points to 'Coding', your arrows point in very different directions. Cosine similarity just measures how close the directions of those arrows are, ignoring how long they are.

cards.title: Words in Action
cards.body: Consider the words 'king' and 'queen'. Because they are closely related concepts, their vectors point in nearly the same direction, resulting in a high cosine similarity of about 0.85. On the other hand, the words 'king' and 'banana' point in very different directions, giving a low cosine similarity of about 0.05.

cards.title: Length Doesn't Matter
cards.body: You might think that longer vectors (words that appear more often in training) are always more similar to other words. But cosine similarity only looks at the angle between vectors, completely ignoring their length. This means rare words can still match perfectly with common words!

cards.title: Direction vs. Length
cards.body: Compare how cosine similarity ignores size differences and only focuses on the direction of meaning.

right.text: Vectors point apart. Low similarity score (near 0.0) even if they are the same length.

left.text: Vectors point the same way. High similarity score (near 1.0) even if one is longer.

exercises.prompt: What cosine similarity score do we get when two word vectors point in the exact same direction?
exercises.explain: When two vectors point in the exact same direction, the angle between them is 0, which yields a cosine similarity value of 1.0 (perfect alignment).

exercises.prompt: True or False: Cosine similarity measures the straight-line distance between the endpoints of two vectors.
exercises.explain: Cosine similarity looks at the angle (direction) of the vectors, not the straight-line distance between their tips.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of cosine similarity.
exercises.sentence: Cosine similarity measures the similarity of two word vectors by checking the [blank] between them.
exercises.explain: Cosine similarity focuses entirely on the angle between the two vector lines, ignoring how far they stretch.

exercises.prompt: Which explanation of cosine similarity was generated by a broken AI model?

options.text: Cosine similarity checks how close two word vectors point in the same direction, ignoring vector length.
Cosine similarity calculates the total length of the two vectors to see which word is longer.
Cosine similarity scores range from -1 to 1, where 1 means the vectors point in the exact same direction.

exercises.explain: The AI model generated an incorrect definition by claiming cosine similarity measures length. Cosine similarity only measures the angle (direction) of the vectors.

exercises.prompt: Imagine you are building a search engine. Why would you choose cosine similarity over regular distance to compare word vectors?

options.text: It adds the coordinates together to find a total score.
It only looks at how long the word vectors are.
It measures the angle between their directions, ignoring length.
It counts how many letters the two words have in common.

exercises.explain: Cosine similarity focuses only on the angle (direction) of the vectors, not how long they are. Because 'Hot' and 'Cold' point in opposite directions, and 'Warm' and 'Sunny' point in a similar direction, cosine similarity is best suited for direction-based closeness.
